channel.title: NYT > Arts
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: 38 Tony Nominees Reveal the Strangest Skills They’ve Picked Up
item.description: The stars of “Giant,” “Fallen Angels,” “The Rocky Horror Show,” “Ragtime” and more prove they’ll go to great lengths to be believable in a role.
item.dc:creator: Dana Scruggs, Michael Paulson, Alexis Soloski, Nicole Herrington, Jolie Ruben and Amanda Webster
item.media:credit: Dana Scruggs for The New York Times

item.title: 2026 Tony Awards Predictions: Who Will Win?
item.description: Expect wins for the musical “Schmigadoon!” and the play “Liberation,” and for the “Ragtime” stars Joshua Henry and Caissie Levy.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) (Play)

item.title: Refik Anadol’s Dataland: You Feel the A.I. Art, and It Feels You Back
item.description: A look inside Dataland in Los Angeles, dedicated entirely to A.I.-generated art. Refik Anadol, its founder, says it’s for human dreamers. Will critics be convinced?
item.dc:creator: Frank Rose and Sela Shiloni
item.category: Los Angeles County Museum of Art
item.media:credit: Sela Shiloni for The New York Times

item.title: Does the World Still Misunderstand M.I.A.?
item.description: A winding interview with the pop globalist about her legacy of art and incitement.
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli, Jon Caramanica and Caroline Tompkins
item.media:credit: Caroline Tompkins for The New York Times

item.title: Before French Open Finals, They’ll Serve Up a Dance
item.description: The choreographer Benjamin Millepied had an unusual brief: To create two short works for the championship matches at Roland Garros.
item.dc:creator: Roslyn Sulcas and Mathieu Richer Mamousse

item.title: Heir Sues to Claim a Klimt Portrait Thought Lost for Decades
item.description: The painting resurfaced in 2024 and a woman contends in her lawsuit that the work is a portrait of her great-aunt that vanished after the Nazi takeover of Austria.
item.dc:creator: Tom Mashberg and Graham Bowley
item.media:credit: Christian Bruna/Associated Press
item.media:description: A portrait by Gustav Klimt, one of his last works, is the subject of a legal battle. It has various titles because of a dispute over which member of an Austrian Jewish family is depicted.

item.title: Met Museum’s ‘Musical Bodies’ Blurs Humans and Instruments
item.description: “Musical Bodies,” a new exhibition at the Metropolitan Museum of Art, revels in the blurry boundaries between humans and instruments.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone and Ye Fan
item.media:credit: Ye Fan for The New York Times

item.title: For America’s Birthday, a Declaration of Independence Whodunit
item.description: The New York Historical thinks it has identified the anonymous printer behind a rare broadside printing of the Declaration made soon after July 4, 1776.
item.dc:creator: Jennifer Schuessler

item.title: Trump Says Lee Greenwood and Christopher Macchio Will Replace Freedom 250 Artists
item.description: The confusion surrounding the concerts planned for the National Mall highlights the partisan tensions around national celebrations of America’s 250th anniversary.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario and Jennifer Schuessler
item.media:credit: Andrew Kelly/Ap Photo/Andrew Kelly
item.media:description: Lee Greenwood performing last month. In his announcement, President Trump called Mr. Greenwood’s song “God Bless the U.S.A.” “one of the greatest hits of all time.”

item.title: ‘Girl, Interrupted’ Review: A Musical That’s a Little Too Tranquilized
item.description: While the 1999 movie went for melodrama, this stage adaptation with songs by Aimee Mann honors the memoir’s coolly clinical prose.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: The cast of “Girl, Interrupted” at the Public Theater in Manhattan, from left: Mia Pak, Sally Shaw, Juliana Canfield, Katherine Reis, Ta’Rea Campbell and King Princess.

item.title: Jonathan Tropper on the ‘Your Friends & Neighbors’ Season 2 Finale
item.description: “We are skewering a value system,” the creator Jonathan Tropper said of the series. “But at the same time, we’re making the show aspirational.”
item.dc:creator: Austin Considine
item.media:credit: OK McCausland for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jonathan Tropper left Westchester County, but his experiences in the New York suburb inform his satirical series “Your Friends & Neighbors.”

item.title: In ‘Can I Be Frank?,’ Imitation Is the Sincerest Form of Tragedy
item.description: Morgan Bassichis, whose solo show “Can I Be Frank?” resurrects an act by Frank Maya, joins others this season who are recreating the works of deceased artists.
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: Morgan Bassichis in the solo show “Can I Be Frank?” at the SoHo Playhouse in Manhattan.

item.title: MoMA’s ‘Universal Westerns’ Retrospective Shows Why They Matter
item.description: A new MoMA retrospective tracks how Universal Pictures used the western to explore changing American morals.
item.dc:creator: Jason Bailey
item.media:credit: NBCUniversal
item.media:description: Peter Fonda in “The Hired Hand” (1971), which he also directed.

item.title: Anthony Head, ‘Buffy the Vampire Slayer’ and ‘Ted Lasso’ Actor, Dies at 72
item.description: The British actor was a mainstay of influential television shows who first found fame as a Nescafe pitchman.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.media:credit: Christian Alminana/Associated Press
item.media:description: Anthony Head in 2010.

item.title: Alan Saret, Sculptor Who Made Clouds of Wire, Dies at 81
item.description: His best-known works were large, airy tangles that suggested tumbleweeds, nests or tulle. If a window was open, they might sway in the breeze.
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich

item.title: Speight Jenkins, Who Brought Adventure to Seattle Opera, Dies at 89
item.description: He had never produced a performance when he was hired as the company’s general director in 1983. But over 31 years (and lots of Wagner) he became beloved.
item.dc:creator: Tim Page

item.title: Five Science Fiction Movies to Stream Now
item.description: In this month’s picks, fatal fungi and killer calamari. Plus, lots of zombies (and a few podcasters).
item.dc:creator: Elisabeth Vincentelli

item.title: Rébecca Chaillon Punished Her Body Onstage for Years. Now, She’s Testing New Limits.
item.description: Rébecca Chaillon’s latest show tackles social stigma by featuring only performers she describes as “fat” — a label she also applies to herself.
item.dc:creator: Laura Cappelle
item.media:credit: Elliott Verdier for The New York Times
item.media:description: “No one is going to hurt themselves like you do,” a member of the production team for Rébecca Chaillon’s new show told her.

item.title: For Jennifer Lopez and Brett Goldstein, Romance Is Everything
item.description: The Bronx-born superstar and the British comedian, who star together in the Netflix comedy “Office Romance,” have more in common than you think.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Spencer
item.media:credit: Sela Shiloni for The New York Times

item.title: 9 New Movies Our Critics Are Talking About This Week
item.description: Whether you’re a casual moviegoer or an avid buff, our reviewers think these films are worth knowing about.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

item.title: 5 Children’s Movies to Stream Now
item.description: This month’s picks include two choices for young sports fans and a summer blockbuster.
item.dc:creator: Dina Gachman
item.category: Emi Martinez: The Kid Who Stops Time (Movie)

item.title: Late Night Sizes Up Trump’s Reflecting-Pool Ambitions
item.description: “One thing we know for sure is that Trump is definitely obsessed with size,” Seth Meyers said of the president’s bragging about the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)
The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)

item.title: ‘Mexico 86’ Review: This Underdog Has Bags of Cash
item.description: Diego Luna plays an ambitious soccer enthusiast who is trying to secure the World Cup for Mexico a second time.
item.dc:creator: Ben Kenigsberg

item.title: ‘Office Romance’ Review: Prepare for Turbulence
item.description: Jennifer Lopez and Brett Goldstein cause professional headaches in this workplace comedy about an airline.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Kennedy

item.title: ‘Scary Movie’ Review: Wayans Brothers Return to the Horror Parody Franchise
item.description: The sixth installment of this low-grade franchise takes a predictable and dismaying turn, even if it feels like the right time to bring the series back.
item.dc:creator: Brandon Yu

item.title: Art Gallery Shows to See in June
item.description: This week in Newly Reviewed, Will Heinrich files a dispatch from Los Angeles covering Charles Ray’s strangely lifelike sculptures, James Harrison’s flowers and a group show! (Exclamation point will make sense).
item.dc:creator: Will Heinrich

item.title: Mister Rogers: Now on YouTube
item.description: On Thursday, Fred Rogers Productions started a channel dedicated to the beloved children’s host. Eventually it will be a repository for full episodes, behind-the-scenes footage and compilations.
item.dc:creator: Sopan Deb
item.category: A Beautiful Day in the Neighborhood (Movie)
item.media:credit: Gene J. Puskar/Associated Press
item.media:description: Fred Rogers on the set of his long-running PBS show, “Mister Rogers’ Neighborhood,” in Pittsburgh in 1989.

item.title: Kennedy Center Tells Staff to Immediately Remove Trump’s Name From Documents
item.description: The center’s general counsel also said that a federal judge’s order meant the president’s name must be taken off outdoor and indoor signage by June 12.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Mandel Ngan/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Parking signage bearing the “Trump Kennedy Center” branding must be altered by June 12, according to an internal memo sent to the institution’s staff, while other documents must be changed immediately.

item.title: Amid Kennedy Center Strife, the National Symphony Faces a Cloudy Future
item.description: After months of uncertainty, and without an approved budget or secured venue, the orchestra has been unable to schedule its next season.
item.dc:creator: Julia Jacobs and Adam Nagourney
item.category: Kennedy, John F, Center for the Performing Arts
item.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gianandrea Noseda, music director of the National Symphony Orchestra, conducting a performance featuring the Washington Chorus in January. The orchestra’s next season is facing uncertainties.

item.title: Wim Wenders Pulls Film With Teenage Nudity After Pleas by Nastassja Kinski
item.description: Nastassja Kinski was 13 years old when she was shown topless in the 1975 film “Wrong Move.” “I could already tell that wasn’t right,” she told a German newspaper.
item.dc:creator: Christopher F. Schuetze
item.media:credit: Michel Lipchitz/Associated Press
item.media:description: Nastassja Kinski and Wim Wenders after a screening of “Paris, Texas” in 1984.

item.title: Limmie Pulliam, Tenor Who Overcame the Stigma of His Size, Dies at 50
item.description: He quit singing in his early 20s when opera companies objected to his appearance, but after a 12-year absence he revived his career to acclaim.
item.dc:creator: Adam Nossiter

item.title: Pommelien Thijs, a Pop Star for Half a Country
item.description: Pommelien Thijs’s catchy tunes are beloved in Belgium — in the Dutch-speaking part, at least.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall and Ilvy Njiokiktjien
item.media:credit: Ilvy Njiokiktjien for The New York Times

item.title: Alan Gribben, Twain Scholar Who Excised Slur From ‘Huck Finn,’ Dies at 84
item.description: He made it his mission to track down every book Mark Twain owned — and to fix what he saw as flaws that kept schools from teaching the author’s most famous works.
item.dc:creator: Michael S. Rosenwald

item.title: A Chance to Shop the Paris Opera’s Jewel-Encrusted Closet
item.description: Vintage ball gowns, potbellied fat suits and diaphanous tunics were among the offerings when the Paris Opera held a public sale of opera and ballet costumes.
item.dc:creator: Roslyn Sulcas
item.media:credit: Julien Mignot for The New York Times
item.media:description: Avalon Alexander traveled to Paris from Malta for the costume sale.

item.title: Where Sky Meets Water, Sanford Biggers Sees Life’s ‘Drift’
item.description: In his first major solo survey on Long Island, the artist explores his connection to the East End’s natural environment and culture in textile works, prints, sculptures and installations.
item.dc:creator: Celia McGee and Kadar R. Small
item.media:description: Sanford Biggers adjusting “Mirror" (2024), a marble sculpture with draped textile, and “Adrift,” 2026, a patterned sand installation created with colored sand poured directly onto the floor, at the Parrish Art Museum.

item.title: Nathan Lane and Laurie Metcalf on Their Tony-Nominated ‘Death of a Salesman’
item.description: The stars Nathan Lane and Laurie Metcalf talk with the show’s director, Joe Mantello, about the exhilaration of collaborating and the trap of sentimentality.
item.dc:creator: Erik Piepenburg
item.media:credit: Ye Fan for The New York Times
item.media:description: From left, Nathan Lane, Joe Mantello and Laurie Metcalf in Lane’s dressing room at the Winter Garden Theater in Manhattan after a recent performance of “Death of a Salesman.”

item.title: ‘Underland’ Review: Subterranean Adventurers
item.description: All over the world, voyagers are descending, legally and not, into the lower depths. This documentary offers a frustratingly abbreviated look at three of them.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: ‘She’s the He’ Review: A Campy, Gender Swap Comedy
item.description: Nico Carney and Misha Osherovich play best friends on a mission to enter the women’s locker room.
item.dc:creator: Chris Azzopardi

item.title: ‘Masters of the Universe’ Review: Becoming a He-Man
item.description: The filmmakers behind this new live-action movie lean into the franchise’s ridiculousness, with sometimes engaging results.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Kenny

item.title: ‘The Little Sister’ Review: Learning to Love
item.description: A French Algerian teenager grapples with her sexuality and her faith in a tender coming-of-age drama.
item.dc:creator: Alissa Wilkinson

item.title: ‘Carolina Caroline’ Review: Hearts on the Run
item.description: Samara Weaving and Kyle Gallner are magnetic in this lovers-on-the-lam joint awash in soulful sincerity.
item.dc:creator: Beatrice Loayza

item.title: ‘Jinsei’ Review: Dreaming of J-Pop Stardom
item.description: In this anime film that follows a taciturn man over decades, the restrained animation is a saving grace.
item.dc:creator: Natalia Winkelman

item.title: Saving Alice’s Wonderland in New York
item.description: “Another Wonderland: Abram Champanier’s Alice Mural” at the Museum of the City of New York brings together 16 panels of the only surviving W.P.A. mural from a hospital children’s ward.
item.dc:creator: Hilarie M. Sheets
item.category: New York City Health and Hospitals Corp
Museum of the City of New York
item.media:credit: Tony Cenicola/The New York Times

item.title: Review: In ‘Another World,’ the Afterlife Glows Red
item.description: The director Tommy Ng Kai Chung turns reincarnation into a visually lush exploration of the human condition.
item.dc:creator: Beandrea July

item.title: Why Do Most New Movies Look Meh?
item.description: Jessica M. Goldstein, an arts and culture reporter, inspects why movies today look different than they did 20 years ago.
item.dc:creator: Jessica M. Goldstein, Edward Vega, Laura Salaberry and Whitney Shefte

item.title: 11 Books That Capture the Swoonworthy Drama of Wedding Season
item.description: Some will have you mentally arranging flowers for your own happy day. Others provide the vicarious thrill of watching it all burn.
item.dc:creator: Jenny Singer
item.media:credit: Maddie Fischer

item.title: Kiyon Ross Wants Dancers at the School of American Ballet on the Edge of Danger
item.description: With “Proof of Light,” Kiyon Ross wants to make his alma mater, the School of American Ballet, proud — and the dancers feel special. That’s what he would have wanted.
item.dc:creator: Gia Kourlas and Yuvraj Khanna
item.media:description: “You can make a shape, but can you dance?” Kiyon Ross said he was drawn to dancers who are expansive and fill the space.

item.title: Late Night Reacts to Another Reality Star Wooing Voters
item.description: Michael Kosta wasn’t impressed to see that the L.A. mayoral candidate Spencer Pratt “got a second-season pickup” after Tuesday’s midterms.
item.dc:creator: Trish Bendix
item.category: The Daily Show with Jon Stewart (TV Program)
Late Night with Seth Meyers (TV Program)

item.title: Pace Gallery Cuts 50 Artists and 50 Staff Amid Art Market Challenges
item.description: A challenging art market is forcing even an established player to contract.
item.dc:creator: Robin Pogrebin
item.media:credit: Clement Pascal for The New York Times
item.media:description: “The whole art gallery art system became too big, too commercial, too impersonal and too corporate,” Marc Glimcher, the chief executive of Pace Gallery, said.

item.title: Ted Danson Apologizes for Blackface Roast of Whoopi Goldberg in 1993
item.description: The actor’s monologue drew condemnation from figures including David N. Dinkins, New York’s first Black mayor.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Malcolm Clarke/Associated Press
item.media:description: Whoopi Goldberg and Ted Danson before the Friars Club roast in 1993.

item.title: The Good List: 6 Things to Bring Delight to Your Day
item.description: Questions for bats, Rothko weather and Knicks fever.
item.dc:creator: Melissa Kirsch

item.title: ‘The Fantasticks’ and ‘Gloria’ Will Head to Broadway This Season
item.description: The nonprofit Second Stage Theater said it would present a reimagined version of “The Fantasticks” and the Pulitzer finalist “Gloria” at the Helen Hayes Theater.
item.dc:creator: Derrick Bryson Taylor
item.media:credit: Sara Krulwich/The New York Times
item.media:description: The 2015 production of Branden Jacobs-Jenkins’s play “Gloria” at the Vineyard Theater.

item.title: John McClain, Who Helped Keep Michael Jackson’s Profits Flowing, Dies at 72
item.description: A music executive with longstanding ties to the Jackson family, he worked to make Janet a star and Michael a posthumous commercial powerhouse.
item.dc:creator: Alex Williams
item.media:credit: Wally Skalij/Los Angeles Times, via Getty Images
item.media:description: John McClain in 1998. He was a producer of the Jackson biopic “Michael,” released in April, which has earned about $850 million worldwide.

item.title: Caissie Levy, Tony Awards Contender and ‘Ragtime’ Star, Is Having a Moment
item.description: Caissie Levy was Broadway’s first Elsa. She starred in “Hair” and “Ghost.” And now, for “Ragtime,” she is an odds-on favorite to win a Tony Award.
item.dc:creator: Michael Paulson and George Etheredge
item.media:credit: George Etheredge for The New York Times

item.title: Artists Threaten to Sue Venice Biennale for Including Them in Awards
item.description: The artists want their names removed from a public vote to decide the best artist and pavilion, awards that are usually decided by a jury of experts.
item.dc:creator: Alex Marshall
item.media:credit: Casey Kelbaugh for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Venice Biennale’s central pavilion last month.

item.title: What’s Next Now That Live Nation Has Been Found to Act as a Monopoly
item.description: The music industry may be significantly changed by the judicial rulings to come, but there is still a lot of lawyering left to do.
item.dc:creator: Ben Sisario
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: Now that a federal jury has found that Live Nation acted as a monopoly, there will likely be months of legal wrangling to determine what damages it will pay or whether the verdict will stand.

item.title: I Tried to Have a Kid With My Best Friend. It Got Complicated.
item.description: Two queer friends dreamed of raising a child together. They almost made it real.
item.dc:creator: Anna Martin, Reva Goldberg, Davis Land, Sara Curtis, Elisa Gutierrez, Lynn Levy, Daniel Ramirez, Dan Powell, Elisheba Ittoop, Marion Lozano and Pat McCusker

item.title: Even on His Own, Nick Is Still a Jonas Brother
item.description: With a starring role in “Power Ballad” and ambitions across mediums, the 33-year-old is carving a singular path.
item.dc:creator: Ashley Spencer and OK McCausland

item.title: At Interlochen, Where Jeffrey Epstein’s Shadow Still Lingers
item.description: The arts school and camp is still contending with the fallout from its former ties to Mr. Epstein, an alumnus and donor accused of preying on two girls he met there.
item.dc:creator: Graham Bowley and Jamie Kelter Davis
item.media:credit: Jamie Kelter Davis for The New York Times

item.title: N.B.A. Finals Introduce Music by Nicholas Britell and Nas
item.description: The league is rolling out music by the composer Nicholas Britell and the rapper Nas that, if successful, could become the sport’s sonic shorthand.
item.dc:creator: Joshua Barone
item.media:credit: Amir Hamja for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sonic dream team: Nas, left, and Nicholas Britell in Brooklyn.

item.title: 5 Minutes That Will Make You Love Charlie Parker
item.description: Bird helped usher in bebop and transformed the alto saxophone into an instrument of startling possibility. Here’s an 11-song primer on his enduring legacy.
item.dc:creator: Marcus J. Moore

item.title: Making Scottish Ballet’s ‘Mary, Queen of Scots’ Legible to Americans
item.description: Scottish Ballet realized it needed to make its nation’s history a bit more explicit to take its “Mary, Queen of Scots” on the road. It comes to Lincoln Center this week.
item.dc:creator: Eric Grode
item.category: Lincoln Center for the Performing Arts
